retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Dominant -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: First, the Law of Dominance and Uniformity states that some alleles, which are variants of a particular gene found at the same chromosomal locus or location, are dominant over the other alleles for a given gene. Those traits that are not dominant are termed recessive. If an organism inherits at least one dominant variant, then it will display the effect, or phenotype, of the dominant allele. [1] Second, the Law of Segregation states that the two alleles for each gene separate from each other during gametogenesis so that the parent may only pass off one allele; thus, the offspring can only inherit one allele from each parent. Third, the Law of Independent Assortment (Law of Reassortment) states that the alleles of different genes segregate independently of one another during gametogenesis and are distributed independently of one another in the next generation. [2] This concept was later verified with chromosomes, though also disproven in some instances. For example, when genes occur on the same chromosome, they can be linked and not follow this law.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Recessive -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Mendelian genetics focuses on disorders caused by single genes, thus excluding polygenic or multifactorial traits. Mendelian genetics has its basis on the experiments of Gregor Mendel, the Austrian monk who worked on garden peas. Mendel described three laws. [1] The first law is the Law of Segregation, which states that each gamete contains one allele for a trait, and it combines with the other gamete to give the offspring a pair of alleles for each trait, one from each parent. The second law is the Law of Independent Assortment, which states that alleles for different traits may segregate and combine independently of each other during meiosis and fertilization. The third law is the Law of Dominance, which states that one allele can be dominant to the other, thereby masking the other if both are present. The masked allele, in this case, is known as the recessive allele.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Dominant -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The field of genetics was born through meticulous studies in a monastery garden by a 19th-century monk, Gregor Mendel. His proposed laws explained the modes of inheritance of characteristic traits passed on through generations, such as the flower color of a pea plant. Though it would be many years before the term gene was introduced and much has been learned since his initial observations, the laws have withstood our advances and understanding of biology, with some interesting exceptions. Gregor Mendel proposed three laws explaining the inheritance of traits visible through generations - the characteristic of pea skin - wrinkled or smooth, the color of a pea plant flower - white, pink, red - among other features. We now understand that these traits are encoded in our instruction manual or our DNA. These simple changes to the phenotype, or the trait displayed in an organism, can be explained through changes in our genes. Mendel's laws include the Law of Dominance and Uniformity, the Law of Segregation, and the Law of Independent Assortment.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Dominant -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Mendel’s laws come to light within human pathologies in the way of single-gene or monogenic disorders. Disorders that follow an autosomal dominant mode of inheritance manifest when an individual inherits at least one dominant allele (A) for a disorder gene. Following Mendel’s Law of Dominance and Uniformity, only one dominant allele within the disease gene is necessary for an individual to be affected or show the phenotype. The classic example is as follows: if one parent is affected with an autosomal dominant disorder, thus heterozygous (Aa), while the other parent is not affected and homozygous (aa), then 50% of the offspring will have the chance of 1) receiving one dominant allele, resulting in the heterozygous (Aa) state and being affected with the disorder or 2) receiving both recessive alleles, resulting in the homozygous (aa) state and not being affected with the disorder. Please see Figure 1 for segregation, as illustrated through a Punnett Square.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Recessive -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell disease also exhibits incomplete dominance and haploinsufficiency.  Individuals who carry a single disease allele (heterozygotes) have an intermediate trait (sickle cell trait) that is phenotypically somewhere between unaffected (homozygous normal allele) and severely affected (homozygous disease allele giving rise to sickle cell anemia). These individuals are not considered completely unaffected, hence incomplete dominance. Hemoglobinopathies are most prevalent among African populations and subtropical populations where malaria is rampant because the carriers of these genes have protection against malaria. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Dominant -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The most expeditious way to determine the autosomal dominant inheritance pattern of a disorder within a family is by analyzing the family pedigree. Since autosomal dominant disorders involve autosomes or the non-sex chromosomes, the disorders affect males and females equally. Also, autosomal dominant disorders rarely skip generations because they only require the inheritance of one dominant allele to express the phenotype of the disorder. The chance of inheriting and expressing the disorder phenotype is dependent on the genotype and phenotype of the parents.

retrieved_docs.title: Supernumerary Digit -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Polydactyly is primarily an autosomal dominant anomaly that exhibits variable penetrance. Polydactyly results from a disruption in digit control during development. [1] Currently, a minimum of 10 genetic loci and 6 genes are recognized as contributors to the non-syndromic form of polydactyly. Limb and digit development is guided by multiple signal centers between 4 and 8 weeks of gestation. Sequence variants in the sonic hedgehog ( SHH ) enhancer ZRS (zone of polarizing activity regulatory sequence) and GLI3 (glioma-associated oncogene homolog 3) cause at least 6 different forms of polydactyly. Variants in the SHH gene are responsible for thumb or preaxial polydactyly type I, the most prevalent form of this condition. Expression of SHH within a region known as the zone of polarizing activity (ZPA) is regulated by the expression of the ZRS and is  responsible for the development of the anterior-posterior (radial-ulnar) axis of the limb. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Dominant -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: As with any heritable single-gene disorder, penetrance plays a role. Penetrance is measured by the percentage of individuals who inherit a disorder allele AND display the phenotype of the disorder. [4] Therefore, all individuals who inherit a disorder allele may not exhibit the phenotype of the disorder (reduced penetrance); however, they can still pass on the allele and have an affected child. Autosomal dominant disorders with a higher penetrance result in more individuals displaying the phenotype who inherit the disorder allele. Furthermore, when age is a factor for the disorder (i.e., it only is apparent in adulthood), the family history may not appear to be dominant if the individuals you are assessing are not yet old enough to display the phenotype.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mosaicism -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Somatic mosaicism may occur as a result of any type of mutation, ranging from chromosomal abnormalities to single nucleotide alterations. Mosaic aneuploidies are generated by two principal mechanisms (Image 2): post-zygotic mitotic non-disjunction or post-zygotic mitotic trisomy rescue after a meiotic non-disjunction. The second indicates a biological attempt to restore the normal chromosome number, which is known as a chromosomal rescue . This mechanism is achieved through the loss of one random supernumerary chromosome in a somatic cell, restoring the euploid state. Nonetheless, chromosome rescue constitutes an increased risk for uniparental disomy, thereby interfering with genomic imprinting and elevating the likelihood of homozygosity for a recessive mutation. [5] [6] [43] Other mechanisms that can cause mosaicism include anaphase lagging and endoreplication. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Human Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The HLA (human leukocyte antigens) complex is located on the short arm of chromosome 6. [1] [2] [3] The HLA genes follow the principles of Mendelian genetics and the encoded antigens are co-dominantly expressed on the cell surface. In the absence of a recombination event, HLA genes are normally inherited en bloc from each parent due to their close proximity resulting in their close physical linkage. The HLA haplotype is a combination of linked HLA genes (HLA-A, -B,-C,-DR, -DQ,-DP) transmitted on a single parental chromosome. [2] HLA antigens are expressed on the surface of many cells and play a major role in self-recognition, evoking the immune response to an antigenic stimulus, and to the orchestration of cellular and humoral immunity. [1]  HLA complex is known to be highly polygenic as it is composed of many genes, which can divide broadly into three categories: Class I, Class II, and Class III. [2] Polymorphism is another feature of the HLA molecule. Polymorphism allows the presence of multiple variations of antigens or alleles. The HLA class I and class II antigens have the most highly polymorphic structural genes found in humans, which allows amino acids in any given HLA molecule to vary slightly from one person to the next. This variation generates distinct HLA types and also causes allograft rejection when tissues are transplanted. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mosaicism -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: Phenotypical expression depends on the number of cells affected. The phenotypical expression may happen during intrauterine life, after birth, or even in late-life on the base of the trigger factors. [30] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Autosomal Dominant -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Though dominant inheritance is common, dominant conditions can occur sporadically or de novo within a family due to sporadic mutation in parental gonads or within the developing fetus. In fact, many autosomal dominant disorder mutations arise de novo , or for the first time within a family, in an affected individual. [3] These  de novo mutations are not inherited from a parent; therefore, siblings of an affected individual are very unlikely to be affected. If the affected individual is heterozygous (Aa) and has a child with an unaffected individual (aa), the children will have a 50% chance of inheriting a dominant allele (Aa) and being affected by the disorder. Germline mosaicism can also play a factor; however, in that, if a parent only has the mutation in their germline, they can have a higher recurrence risk (50% rather than <1%).

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mosaicism -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic mosaicism is defined as the presence of two or more cell lineages with different genotypes arising from a single zygote in a single individual. In contrast, if distinct cell lines derived from different zygotes, the term is now known as chimerism. Genetic mosaicism is a postzygotic mutation. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mosaicism -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The early embryo is susceptible to mitotic errors due to the inactivation of the genome at fertilization. During the first three cell divisions, the embryo is dependent on oocyte cytoplasmic transcriptomes. Embryonic genome activation initiates after the third cleavage-stage; even some genes that encode critical proteins for cell division do not express until the blastocyst stage.  Accordingly, laboratory detection of mosaicism has been reported as elevated as 70 and 90% of all cleavage- and blastocyst-stage embryos that derive from in vitro fertilization, respectively. [36] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mosaicism -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The distribution and phenotypical findings largely depend on the precise time in which the mutation occurs during embryonic development. If a mutation arises during initial mitosis after fertilization, approximately half of all the individual’s cells will harbor the new mutation, which may manifest as a demarcation of affected and unaffected tissue along the midline. Likewise, although the exact moment of left-right separation in humans is unknown, mutation occurring after this dissociation present with affected cells and tissues that are less likely to cross the midline. Finally, mutations that occur before primordial germ cell differentiation (approximately before 15 mitotic divisions) may be present in both somatic and germ tissues. Otherwise, if emerging after this period, abnormal cells are confined to either somatic or germinal lineages. Therefore, establishing the timing of a mutation may suggest the proportion of mutant cells and, consequently, the potential recurrence risk for transmission of the same mutation to additional offspring. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1439
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic disturbances that encompass specific conditions such as اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي and فرط البروتينات الشحمية. This category also includes the clinical concept ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) as one of its members, indicating a link between lipid abnormalities and early‑onset hyperglycemia. In classification systems, disorders of lipid metabolism are mapped to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ, a form of familial hyperlipidemia. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that the trait is transmitted through a single copy of the altered gene from an affected parent. Together, these relationships highlight how lipid metabolism disorders are interconnected with other metabolic disorders, specific disease entities, and genetic inheritance mechanisms, informing both diagnostic categorization and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary disorder of nervous system is a broad category of genetic conditions affecting the nervous system, and شقيقة فالجية عائلية (familial hemiplegic migraine) is a specific instance within this category. شقيقة فالجية عائلية represents a hereditary form of migraine that is characterized by transient hemiplegia and other neurological symptoms, illustrating how a hereditary disorder of the nervous system can manifest as a distinct migraine subtype.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: Autosomal dominant inheritance is the mode of transmission for فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (hyperlipidemia type IIa). This genetic pattern means that a single copy of the altered gene inherited from either parent is sufficient to cause the disorder. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is characterized by markedly elevated low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, which increase the risk of premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Because the condition follows an autosomal dominant pattern, it often appears in successive generations, and first‑degree relatives of an affected individual have a 50 % chance of inheriting the same lipid abnormality. Recognizing the autosomal dominant inheritance of فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is essential for family screening, early diagnosis, and implementation of lipid‑lowering therapies to reduce cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1062
retrieved_docs.content: within nervous system disorders. Clinically, شقيقة and شقيقة فالجية عائلية are considered clinically similar, and شقيقة and صداع وعائي are described as related and possibly synonymous, suggesting substantial overlap in their clinical presentation and underlying mechanisms. Together, these relationships integrate migraine into a network of genetic, vascular, and systemic neurological conditions, underscore its diagnostic and therapeutic considerations, and illustrate its connections to both broader headache classifications and specific hereditary migraine phenotypes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism encompass conditions such as فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia). Within this broader category, فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is a specific manifestation that is mapped to the class of lipid‑metabolism disorders. Type IIa hyperlipidemia follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that Autosomal dominant inheritance is the genetic transmission mechanism for this particular disorder. Recognizing that فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is a form of فرط البروتينات الشحمية and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians diagnose, screen families, and manage patients with lipid metabolism abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 669
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are recognized as a specific form of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and are also classified under hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). Within this category, elevated blood glucose and new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)) are considered member conditions, highlighting the close link between lipid abnormalities and early disturbances in carbohydrate metabolism. Laboratory results that are “above reference range” are interpreted as indicative of a disorder of lipid metabolism, underscoring the diagnostic relevance of lipid panels. The disorder “فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ” (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is mapped to the broader concept of disorders of lipid metabolism and is known to follow an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, indicating that a single copy of the pathogenic allele can transmit the condition. Moreover, carbohydrate metabolism disorders (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات) are positioned as a parent category to metabolic disorders (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي), reflecting the hierarchical organization of metabolic disease entities. Together, these relationships illustrate how lipid metabolism disorders intersect with broader metabolic pathways, genetic inheritance, and clinical interpretation, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

An eye‑related subtype, “الشقيقة الشالة للعين,” is recorded as a child of شقيقة and as a descendant of the cranial‑nerve disorder “اضطراب بالعصب القحفي,” reflecting a specific manifestation that involves ocular symptoms. Genetic and hereditary forms are captured by “Hereditary disorder of nervous system,” which is inversely related to the familial migraine entity “شقيقة فالجية عائلية.” These two migraine variants are noted as clinically similar, underscoring their shared pathophysiology despite possible differences in inheritance patterns. In the context of cerebrovascular disease, the rare condition “CEREBRAL ARTERIOPATHY, AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT, WITH SUBCORTICAL INFARCTS AND LEUKOENCEPHALOPATHY, TYPE 1” lists شقيقة as one of its manifestations, linking migraine to subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy. retrieved_docs.content: syndrome is identified as a chronic nervous system disorder and includes شقيقة as one of its child conditions, indicating that migraine can be viewed as a chronic mixed headache presentation. Familial hemiplegic migraine (شقيقة فالجية عائلية) is clinically similar to شقيقة and is categorized as a hereditary disorder of the nervous system, reflecting its genetic basis. Both شقيقة and شقيقة فالجية عائلية share clinical features and may be considered synonymous or closely related within the vascular headache spectrum. CEREBRAL ARTERIOPATHY, AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT, WITH SUBCORTICAL INFARCTS AND LEUKOENCEPHALOPATHY, TYPE 1 is described as a manifestation of اضطراب المِشْيَة (gait disorder) and also presents with شقيقة, linking cerebrovascular disease to migraine‑type headache symptoms. This underscores the importance of recognizing migraine as a possible neurological manifestation of systemic vascular disorders. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base links migraine (شقيقة) to multiple hierarchical and clinical contexts. شقيقة is classified as a child of Chronic mixed headache syndrome, a chronic nervous system disorder, and serves as a parent concept for the broader Migraine and Headache Syndromes category, indicating its role both as a specific headache type and an umbrella term. Familial hemiplegic migraine (شقيقة فالجية عائلية) is an inverse‑isa of Hereditary disorder of the nervous system, connecting this migraine variant to genetic nervous system diseases. Migraine is also a member of Neurological Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term), a specific form of vascular headache (صداع وعائي), and is placed under the general headache (صداع) and headache syndrome (syndromy bolesti hlavy) categories, which are associated with cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) changes (ثَرٌّ أَنْفِي بالسائل الدماغي النخاعي). Variants include ocular migraine (الشقيقة الشالة للعين), which encompasses classic migraine and is linked to

Overall, migraine (شقيقة) is interconnected with hereditary, vascular, and structural cerebral disorders, is classified across several hierarchical and synonymous relationships, and serves as both a specific clinical entity and a bridge to broader central nervous system disease concepts, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, genetic counseling, and comprehensive patient management.

Diabetic, idiopathic progressive, malignancy‑associated, collagen‑vascular disease, and severe polyneuropathies are all classified as peripheral neuropathies. “اعتلال مرتكز العظم” (bone‑centered neuropathy) is linked to tendon‑sheath inflammation, indicating an interaction between osseous neural changes and tenosynovial inflammation. Collectively, the hierarchy and relational network portray “Other disorders of nervous system” as a comprehensive diagnostic framework that integrates neuropathic pain, focal nerve lesions, muscle‑nerve disorders, hereditary and idiopathic neuropathies, toxic/inflammatory/vascular neuropathies, drug‑induced and metabolic polyneuropathies, chronic demyelinating diseases, genetic neuropathies, and distinct syndromes.
